taxonomy.differentiation_note: Crypto infrastructure ≠ fintech payments in two fundamental ways: (1) revenue driver — fintech payments derive revenue from transaction volume flowing through established card networks and banking rails (Visa, Mastercard, Adyen); crypto infrastructure derives revenue primarily from crypto asset price level and volatility, spot/derivatives trading fees on centralised exchanges (Coinbase Q1 2026: $1.4B revenue, ~75% from transaction fees tied to crypto market volumes), and Bitcoin mining economics (block subsidy + fees, which are functions of BTC price and hashrate, not merchant payment volumes); (2) regulatory regime and counterparty risk — fintech payments are regulated under PSD2/PCI-DSS frameworks with established consumer protection; crypto infrastructure operates under an evolving CFTC/SEC/CLARITY Act regime with material legal execution risk still embedded in valuations. Additionally, crypto infrastructure ≠ semiconductors despite miners consuming substantial compute hardware: the economic driver of miners is the Bitcoin protocol's halving schedule and network difficulty, not silicon supply/demand or AI chip allocations. The equity value of MARA or Riot Platforms is a function of BTC price and their AISC ($/BTC mined), not fab capacity or TSMC lead times.

demand_drivers: Bitcoin price cycle — the single dominant demand driver for exchange volumes and miner economics. Bitcoin trading at ~$99,887 as of June 2026 (market cap ~$1.98T). BTC 11% down YTD in 2026 but +~50% vs June 2025. At current BTC price, miner revenue per TH/s (hashprice) approximately $0.06-0.07/day, compressed vs $0.12/day pre-halving April 2024 but supportable for efficient operators at ~34 W/T average network efficiency.

Regulatory clarity structural inflection — GENIUS Act (stablecoins) signed July 2025 ended a decade of US regulatory ambiguity on stablecoins. CLARITY Act (Digital Asset Market Clarity Act) introduced in Senate Banking Committee May 2026 (278-page draft), creating a federal framework classifying tokens as securities vs commodities and establishing exchange/custodian standards. This is the most material structural catalyst: regulatory clarity enables institutional capital to deploy into crypto infrastructure equities that were previously compliance-blocked. Coinbase directly benefits as the regulated onramp.

Institutional Bitcoin ETF adoption — US spot Bitcoin ETPs approved January 2024 drove record $18.7B institutional inflows in Q1 2026. BlackRock IBIT alone accumulated $54B AUM. April 2026 saw $2.44B monthly inflows (strongest since October 2025). Analysts project >$180B total US spot BTC ETF AUM by end-2026. Exchange volumes are structurally elevated as ETF arbitrage, custody, and prime brokerage flows route through regulated exchanges.

Bitcoin halving supply shock (April 2024) — Block subsidy cut from 6.25 to 3.125 BTC/block. With ~1.32 million BTC remaining to mine (<7% of total supply) and ~3-4M BTC permanently lost, supply inflation is structurally declining. Historically, halving events have preceded 12-18 month bull cycles as supply issuance falls while institutional demand holds or rises. The 2024 halving effect should be fully absorbed into price by Q3-Q4 2026.

AI/HPC pivot by miners — MARA (72.2 EH/s hashrate, +33% YoY as of Q1 2026) and Riot Platforms ($33.2M data center revenue in Q1 2026, AMD exercised option for 25 MW HPC capacity) are converting idle mining infrastructure into AI high-performance computing colocation. This diversifies revenue away from pure BTC price dependency and re-rates miners from commodity-sensitive to infrastructure-sensitive multiples — a structural demand driver for miner equities independent of crypto prices.

Stablecoin payment rail adoption — GENIUS Act creates a federal licensing framework enabling banks and non-banks to issue dollar-pegged stablecoins. Stablecoin transaction volumes growing ~40% YoY. Exchange fee revenue from stablecoin trading pairs (USDT, USDC) provides a lower-volatility revenue floor for exchanges even in bear markets.
supply_constraints: Post-halving miner margin compression — block reward at 3.125 BTC/block since April 2024. Network hashrate at 72+ EH/s (MARA alone). Higher difficulty = higher cost per BTC mined. Inefficient miners (>40 W/T) are below breakeven at BTC < $90,000; only operators at ~34 W/T or below remain profitable at current BTC price ($99,887) with network difficulty at all-time highs. Supply of profitable mining capacity is therefore constrained to large well-capitalised operators.

Energy cost and access — Bitcoin mining is the most energy-intensive compute workload per unit of revenue. Large miners require 100s of MW of stranded or cheap power. Competition for power sites from AI data centers (1-3 MW per rack vs mining's lower density) is intensifying — Riot's Corsicana pivot illustrates both the opportunity and the tension. New mining capacity faces 12-24 month lead times for power interconnection.

Regulatory operating restrictions — Jurisdictions continue to restrict or ban proof-of-work mining (EU MiCA discussion, New York State moratorium). US remains the largest mining jurisdiction (~40% of global hashrate) but state-level permitting adds friction. Mining equities bear regulatory jurisdiction risk not present in ETPs.

Exchange license concentration — Coinbase holds the dominant US regulated exchange position but faces global competition from Binance (non-US), Kraken, and OKX. New exchange entrants face regulatory barriers that protect incumbents but also limit the universe of investable equities (few liquid exchange ETFs exist at the sector level).

Bitcoin protocol's fixed 21M cap — not a supply constraint on equities, but the finite supply underpins the scarcity narrative. The 28-year mining tail (final BTC mined ~2140) means transaction fee revenue must eventually replace block subsidies — a long-term miner business model risk.

cycle_position.assessment: Mid-cycle bull market recovery — post-consolidation, not yet late-cycle euphoria
cycle_position.rationale: Bitcoin at ~$99,887 (June 2026), ~11% below Jan 2026 high but well above the $69K-$82K range of Q4 2025. The Crypto Fear & Greed Index sits at 11 (Extreme Fear) as of early June 2026 following 6 consecutive days of ETF outflows ($1.26B) after April's record inflows — a classic mid-cycle flush, not a cycle top. The Jan 2024 US spot ETF approval was the structural regime change; the post-halving (April 2024) supply shock is the cyclical tailwind. Historical pattern: BTC halving cycles produce price peaks ~12-18 months post-event, suggesting cycle peak in Q4 2026-Q1 2027 if the pattern holds. Exchange equities (COIN) and miner equities (MARA, RIOT) demonstrate >2x leverage to BTC price moves — April 2026 saw MARA +30%+, COIN +20% on a BTC recovery. Current positioning is cautious (extreme fear) while fundamentals (regulatory clarity, institutional ETF AUM, AI/HPC pivot) are constructively improving. Assessment: entry window into infrastructure equities is more attractive at current sentiment levels than at peak, but BTC price trajectory remains the non-negotiable variable.

key_metrics_to_monitor.comment: Primary driver of all crypto infrastructure equity valuations. Above $90K sustains large-miner profitability. Above $110K unlocks significant margin expansion at current network difficulty.
Proxy for network security and mining industry capex. MARA alone at 72.2 EH/s as of Q1 2026 (+33% YoY). Rising hashrate compresses margins per miner; watch vs BTC price for hashprice sensitivity.

Post-halving hashprice dropped from $0.12 (April 2024) to ~$0.049 (April 2025); recovered to ~$0.06-0.07 in Q1-Q2 2026 as BTC recovered. Break-even for efficient operators (~34 W/T) is approximately $0.04-0.05/TH/s/day at current energy prices.

key_metrics_to_monitor.metric: US spot Bitcoin ETF net flows (weekly)
key_metrics_to_monitor.current: Q1 2026: +$18.7B cumulative; May 2026 saw 6-day outflow streak of -$1.26B
key_metrics_to_monitor.comment: Institutional demand barometer. Sustained weekly outflows > $500M signal institutional de-risking. BlackRock IBIT AUM at $54B is the key anchor. ETF flows lead exchange volumes by 1-2 days.

Q1 2026: $1.4B total revenue, -21% QoQ (vs Q4 2025) on lower crypto market volumes. YoY revenue growth still positive (+9% in 2025). Most important exchange revenue proxy — reflects spot volumes and fee compression trend.

key_metrics_to_monitor.current: Senate Banking Committee draft (May 2026), not yet floor vote
key_metrics_to_monitor.comment: Binary regulatory catalyst. Senate Banking Committee released 278-page draft May 11, 2026. Floor vote timing unknown. Full passage materially expands addressable institutional capital for exchanges and custodians.

etf_analysis.name: Bitwise Physical Bitcoin ETP (formerly ETC Group)
etf_analysis.characteristics: 100% physically backed BTC held in cold-storage custody. Priced in EUR on XETRA — best EU access point for Bitcoin price exposure without an exchange account. AUM ~685-709M EUR as of April-June 2026 (~$740M USD). Not UCITS — structured as an ETP (Exchange Traded Product / ETC) under German law, not a UCITS SICAV. Accessible to Spanish retail investors via XETRA on most broker platforms.
etf_analysis.limitations: NOT UCITS — this is an ETP/ETC, not a UCITS fund. Spanish MIFID II obligations require brokers to confirm appropriateness. No dividend. Pure BTC beta — does not provide exposure to exchange equities or mining companies. TER 2.0% is high vs US ETFs (IBIT: 0.25%) but unavoidable for EU-domiciled BTC access. AUM at $740M passes the $200M liquidity floor. Spread at 12bps is within the 25bps warning threshold. Fee war ongoing in EU ETP space — TER may compress. PREFERRED EU VEHICLE for BTC exposure despite non-UCITS status.

etf_analysis.name: Strategy Inc. (MicroStrategy) — levered BTC equity proxy
etf_analysis.characteristics: Not an ETF. Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy) holds ~214,000+ BTC on its balance sheet financed through equity and convertible debt issuance. Trades at a premium/discount to its BTC NAV depending on market sentiment. Premium reflects optionality and capital markets access; discount signals leverage risk. Monitor as BTC sentiment thermometer — MSTR premium/discount to BTC NAV is one of the best crowding indicators in the sector.
etf_analysis.limitations: NOT AN ETF. Single-stock equity risk. Levered BTC — amplifies both upside and downside vs BTC. Convertible debt obligations create liquidation risk if BTC price falls significantly. Not accessible in UCITS format. Use only as a sentiment/positioning indicator. DO NOT size as a position vehicle — use BTCE.DE for BTC exposure instead.

risks: BTC price cycle reversal — crypto infrastructure equities have 2-3x beta to Bitcoin. An 18% YTD BTC decline as of June 2026 has already compressed MARA/Riot revenues and triggered Q1 2026 miner BTC sales (Riot sold 3,778 BTC; MARA similarly liquidating holdings) to fund operations. A break below $80,000 BTC would push inefficient miners below breakeven and compress Coinbase trading volumes by 30-40% based on historical correlation.

Regulatory binary — CLARITY Act failure or delay — The Senate Banking Committee draft (May 2026) includes contentious stablecoin yield provisions. Failure to pass or a significantly watered-down bill would remove the largest structural re-rating catalyst for crypto infrastructure equities. Estimated 6-9 month delay risk at minimum given Congressional calendar. This is not a terminal risk but would defer the institutional capital unlock.

AI/HPC pivot execution risk for miners — MARA and Riot's pivot to high-performance computing data center colocation is untested at scale. AMD's 25 MW option at Riot (Q1 2026) is a positive data point but represents <15% of total capacity. If HPC contracts fail to ramp, miners revert to pure BTC price dependency with a higher fixed cost base (data center infrastructure capex). Riot's $167M Q1 revenue included $33M HPC — the remaining 80% is still mining-dependent.

ETF outflow regime shift — Bitcoin ETF inflows ($18.7B Q1 2026) are the primary institutional demand signal. The 6-day outflow streak in May 2026 (-$1.26B) following April's record inflows illustrates institutional sensitivity to macro factors (Fed rate hike concerns, BTC failing as geopolitical hedge). If the macro regime turns broadly risk-off (equity bear market), correlation-driven ETF outflows could accelerate — BTC has not yet proven it is a reliable uncorrelated hedge during equity drawdowns.

Mining centralisation and 51% attack concern — Network hashrate increasingly concentrated among 4-5 large listed miners (MARA, Riot, CleanSpark, Bitfarms). If top-3 miners control >33% of hashrate collectively, network security narrative risk rises — historically a negative price catalyst. Currently below risk threshold but watch post-consolidation.

Spanish tax treatment of crypto assets — AEAT classifies crypto assets as a separate category with full CGT application (same 19-27% progressive brackets as financial assets from 2026). However, indirect exposure via ETPs (BTCE.DE) is taxed as a financial investment without the additional crypto reporting requirements (Modelo 721 for assets held outside Spain). Thesis using MSTR (US equity) requires Spanish US-source income declaration. Use BTCE.DE to keep within standard MIFID II investment reporting framework.

historical_catalyst_performance.summary: Crypto infrastructure equities exhibit 3 historically recurring catalyst-return patterns. (1) Regulatory approval events: SEC spot Bitcoin ETF approval (January 10, 2024) drove COIN +40% in 3 weeks, MARA +60%, RIOT +55% — exchanges and miners front-ran the institutional flow narrative by 2-4 weeks before actual inflows materialised. (2) Bitcoin halving events: the April 2024 halving initially compressed miner revenues by 50% (block reward halved) but was followed by a 12-18 month bull cycle — MARA and Riot both saw 3-5x returns from May 2024 through January 2025 as BTC recovered to $105K. Historical halving cycles (2016, 2020, 2024) show consistent 12-18 month lag between halving and peak price. (3) Bitcoin price floor confirmation: at each BTC cycle low, crypto infrastructure equities trough at 40-60% discounts to their BTC-NAV replacement value — historically the best entry signal. Current Fear & Greed Index at 11 (Extreme Fear, June 2026) is structurally similar to October 2023 ($27K BTC, pre-ETF approval) and February 2022 ($37K, pre-bear bottom) — both preceded 40-70% sector rallies within 6 months. The most reliable alpha signal in this sector is identifying regulatory catalysts 2-4 weeks before mainstream announcement, as institutional capital flows immediately on event confirmation.

btc_halving_2024.event: Bitcoin halving April 19, 2024 — block reward 6.25 → 3.125 BTC
btc_halving_2024.miner_response: MARA hashrate +33% YoY to 72.2 EH/s by Q1 2026. Initial miner margin compression then gradual recovery as BTC recovered. Industry shift: only ~34 W/T operators remain profitable at $90-100K BTC.
btc_halving_2024.exchange_response: Coinbase net revenue grew 9% YoY 2025. Q1 2026 revenue $1.4B (-21% QoQ) on lower market volumes — volume highly cyclical.
btc_halving_2024.key_lesson: Halving compresses mining margins short-term but is historically bullish for BTC price 12-18 months later. Exchanges benefit from cycle-high volumes — the timing mismatch between miner pain and exchange gain is exploitable.

sec_etf_approval_jan2024.event: SEC approves 11 spot Bitcoin ETPs simultaneously, January 10, 2024
sec_etf_approval_jan2024.sector_response: COIN +38% over 3 weeks post-approval. Bitcoin from ~$42K to ~$73K by March 2024 (+74%). MARA and Riot +50-80% in same window.
sec_etf_approval_jan2024.institutional_inflows: Q1 2026 cumulative US spot BTC ETF AUM: ~$180B (analyst projection). BlackRock IBIT alone at $54B by June 2026.
sec_etf_approval_jan2024.key_lesson: Regulatory approval events are the single highest-conviction entry catalysts in this sector. Pre-event positioning (when approval probability is rising but not priced) produced the best risk/reward. Post-approval rallies typically run 4-8 weeks before consolidating.

narrative_notes: Classified as MAINSTREAM (score_equiv: 60) rather than crowded. Rationale: Bitcoin and crypto infrastructure receive daily Bloomberg/FT/WSJ coverage, Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan publish crypto market reports, and ETF AUM exceeds $500M in theme (IBIT alone at $54B). These observable criteria clearly exceed the emerging threshold. However, not yet CROWDED because: (1) institutional Bitcoin ETF adoption is still early — Q1 2026 inflows of $18.7B are large but the Fear & Greed Index at 11 (Extreme Fear) indicates retail positioning is fearful, not greedy; (2) the CLARITY Act has not yet passed — the primary institutional re-rating catalyst is not yet priced in; (3) sell-side consensus is constructive but not unanimously bullish — debate about BTC as macro hedge is active; (4) crypto infrastructure equities (COIN, MARA, RIOT) are not in the top-10 most crowded hedge fund themes (AI semiconductors hold that title). The narrative is mainstream-level known but the position sizing is not yet at crowded levels. Narrative_trend: stable — coverage is persistent but not accelerating into a new euphoric phase given BTC's 11% YTD decline and the ETF outflow episode in May 2026.
